text:
3. **Supersession no longer deletes agent-derived claims.** When something is contradicted/superseded, the old claim is *kept as history* regardless of who authored it — same treatment as user claims. So retention becomes **symmetric** (keep the history either way); the only difference agent-vs-user makes is the recorded mark.

If that's right, it's a genuinely good move: **no dilution** (nothing labeled in-guide), **no archaeology loss** (nothing silently deleted — which fixes the exact "agent *fact* that evolved gets erased" risk we hit, since 81% of agent claims turned out to be facts), and **provenance is still preserved** in metadata for later use. [...] B = keep everything -- incuding when the user corrects the model explicitly [...] - **Nothing is ever deleted** — keep-all, *including when you explicitly correct the model*. Supersession keeps the old claim in the log (marked superseded + origin); the guide shows the live tip.
- **The tag's only job = a review filter** — a `wiki doctor`/audit pass surfaces agent-derived claims (esp. never-user-confirmed) for you to audit. No rendering, no deletion, no promotion lifecycle. [...] ## One nuance I *inferred* — please confirm or correct

I had to make a rendering call the spec now records as my interpretation: **superseded *agent* claims are kept in the log but NOT auto-rendered as "Previously: X" breadcrumbs** — only genuine **user mind-changes** render breadcrumbs. Reason: an agent claim might have been wrong/never-true (a corrected hallucination), and rendering "previously X" would falsely imply the system once *was* X. So agent history is retained-for-audit, not presented as live archaeology. If you'd rather *all* superseded claims render breadcrumbs, say so and I'll flip it.
 [...] User: superseeded notes that come from the agent can just be dropped from the source of truth once they become superseeded -- supersseded notes from the user should continue to carry throughout the guide -- if something used to work as A and it started to work as B it's ok, even desirable, for the guide to include a nudge to how it used to work (imagine if the refactor of the code didn't completely catch everything and now we have seemingly slightly weird code, but the context of how it used to work before would explain it and provide context (exactly what we want this project to do!) on how things had been implemented in some way

Assistant: Yes — and this is a genuinely important refinement, because it ties the retention rule to the *whole point of the project*. Let me sharpen it.

## The rule you're describing

Supersession isn't one policy — it's **authority-dependent**:

- **Agent-authority claim, superseded → drop** from the source of truth. It was an executor's hypothesis; once replaced it has no lasting value.
- **User-authority claim, superseded → keep, rendered as provenance.** The guide's live tip is the current state (B), but it carries a compact "was A until session N — changed because X" breadcrumb.

And the clean version of "authority" composes with the admission gate you already have: **user-authority = user-authored OR user-ratified.** (A ratified agent claim became user intent, so *its* supersession also deserves to carry as history. An unratified agent claim never entered the spec in the first place, so there's nothing to drop.)

## Why this is the right call, not just a nicety

You named the actual mission: **the wiki exists to explain why the code looks the way it does.** A refactor that went A→B but didn't fully land leaves "weird" code that's *only* explicable if you know it used to be A. Current-tip-only projection throws away exactly the archaeological context that makes proactive-context valuable. So: [...] **The reconciliation worked — this is the whole point:**
- Session A created `redis-session-store` saying *"sessions stored in Redis."*
- Session B **revised in place** (`op revise`, not append): the guide now reads *"Sessions are stored in a Postgres table. Redis is not used... **(Previously: ...stored in Redis...)**"* — the new decision is current, the old one is **superseded with a `Previously:` archaeology breadcrumb** (your §6 retention rule, working on a real user mind-change).
- The reworded throttling topic **routed back to the same guide** and merged (no duplicate slug) — dedup working.
- All 3 assertions pass via semantic glm judge: topic in exactly 1 guide, Postgres current, Redis not-current.
 [...] Assistant: Just my shorthand for a small provenance note — nothing fancier than that.

A **breadcrumb** is a terse line kept *inside* a guide when a decision gets superseded, recording what it *used to be* and (ideally) why it changed — so the guide shows the current state but doesn't erase the history of how it got there.

Concretely, from the actual e2e test run (a Redis→Postgres reversal):

> ## Session Store
> Sessions are stored in a Postgres table. Redis is not used for session storage. **(Previously: all user sessions were stored in Redis.)**

The live statement is the current truth (Postgres); the bold parenthetical is the breadcrumb. It's:
- **inline**, attached to the claim it concerns (not a separate changelog),
- **terse** — one clause, not the full history,
- **kept only for meaningful evolutions**, not every tweak.

**Why they matter** (the whole point of keeping them): they explain *why present-day code looks the way it does*. If a refactor went A→B but didn't fully land, you're left with code that only makes sense if you know it used to be A. The breadcrumb is that "it used to be A" context — exactly the kind of nuance the wiki exists to preserve.

And the connection to what we were just discussing: the *delete-vs-breadcrumb* decision is the one place the explicit/implicit tag still does real work even if you stop rendering it — a contradicted **agent proposal** gets silently deleted (no breadcrumb; it was never real intent), while a **user mind-change** keeps a breadcrumb (genuine product history). My suggestion was to lean toward *keeping* a breadcrumb whenever you're unsure, since they're rare and cheap, rather than risk silently deleting a real piece of history.
